title: Troubleshooting a compost heap
subtitle: Diagnosing wet, dry, smelly, and inactive heaps from symptoms
excerpt: Most compost problems come from an imbalance between carbon and nitrogen, too much or too little moisture, or insufficient aeration. Each problem produces a distinct symptom. This guide gives a symptom-by-cause-by-fix structure for the eight most common compost failures.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic troubleshooting guidance. RHS composting faults and remedies (rhs.org.uk). WRAP compost quality guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Carbon-to-nitrogen ratio. The proportion of carbon to nitrogen in compost materials. The target for active decomposition is 25–30:1. Browns are carbon-rich (high C:N); greens are nitrogen-rich (low C:N).

Browns are carbon-rich materials: cardboard, straw, wood chip, dry leaves (C:N 80–400:1). Greens are nitrogen-rich: grass clippings, food scraps, fresh plant material (C:N 15–25:1). Balance both to reach the target C:N.

content.text: Compost decomposition requires the right balance of carbon and nitrogen (the
), adequate moisture, and sufficient air. Tip any of these too far in one direction and the process either slows, stalls, or produces the wrong gases. Symptoms map reliably to causes.
Wet and slimy, smells of ammonia

(nitrogen excess). Ammonia is the result of excess nitrogen breaking down without enough carbon to absorb it. Fix: add a thick layer of torn cardboard, wood chip, or straw. Turn the heap to introduce air.
Wet and slimy, smells of sulphur (rotten eggs)

Cause: anaerobic conditions, the heap has compacted or become waterlogged, cutting off oxygen. Sulphur compounds are produced by anaerobic bacteria. Fix: turn the heap thoroughly to restore air pockets. Add dry browns. If the base is waterlogged, fork drainage channels into the soil beneath or relocate the heap.

Cause: too dry, or too many browns and not enough greens to provide nitrogen. The heap needs moisture and nitrogen to support bacterial activity. Fix: water the heap until it feels like a wrung-out sponge. Add nitrogen-rich material: grass clippings, fresh vegetable peelings, coffee grounds.
Not heating up (for a hot compost attempt)

Cause: insufficient volume (minimum 1 m³ needed to self-insulate), incorrect C:N ratio, or insufficient moisture. Fix: check the heap has reached the minimum volume. Add nitrogen material to reach a roughly 25:1 C:N ratio. Ensure moisture is adequate throughout.
Fruit flies: attracted to exposed fresh food. Always cover food additions with a layer of browns.
Rats and mice: attracted by cooked food, meat, fish, or dairy. Never add these to an open heap. Use a rodent-resistant bin with a secured base, or switch to bokashi for cooked food.
Ants: signal the heap is too dry. Water and turn.

Cause: material added in large pieces takes longer to break down and leaves visible chunks in the finished compost. Woody stems, whole cardboard sections, and large vegetable scraps are the usual culprits. Fix for future batches: chop, shred, or tear browns before adding. Sieve the finished compost and return the coarse fraction to the next heap.
